text: The lake was perfectly still at dawn and she sat on the dock with her feet in the water, watching a heron stand motionless in the shallows
He folded the warm laundry slowly, matching corners of towels, stacking shirts by color, the dryer clicking as it cooled in the background
The tea was exactly the right temperature, not too hot, and she held the mug with both hands and watched the rain slide down the window in long uneven lines
The cat was asleep on his lap and the book was open on the arm of the chair and the house was so quiet he could hear the clock in the kitchen two rooms away
She floated on her back in the pool after everyone else had gone inside, looking up at the first stars appearing one by one in the darkening sky
The drive through the countryside was flat and straight and the fields on both sides were the same shade of green and the radio played something instrumental with no commercials
He sat in the waiting room and instead of checking his phone he just watched the light move across the floor as a cloud passed and the sun returned
The bread dough was rising under a cloth on the counter and she sat at the table reading the newspaper with nowhere to be for the next two hours
After the guests left she stood in the kitchen washing the dishes by hand even though the dishwasher was empty, the warm water running over her wrists
The hammock swayed slightly in a wind that smelled like cut grass and he let the paperback rest on his chest and closed his eyes and listened to the sprinkler tick across the lawn
Snow was falling straight down with no wind, the flakes large and slow, and the street was empty and white and the only sound was the muffled crunch of his boots
She knitted another row without counting, her fingers knowing the pattern by now, the yarn moving through them like water through a familiar channel
The garden hose made a low sound as he watered the tomatoes in the evening light and the soil darkened in circles around each plant
He walked the same path through the woods he had walked a thousand times, every root and stone familiar, the canopy overhead filtering the sunlight into coins on the ground
The fish tank hummed and the bubbles rose in a steady column and the neon tetras moved together like a single organism turning and turning in their small bright school
She sat cross-legged on the floor sorting old photographs into piles without any urgency, picking each one up and holding it for a moment before placing it gently in a stack
The monks were chanting in a low drone that filled the stone room and he sat on the wooden bench and let the sound move through him like warm air through an open window
At 5 AM the bakery was lit up but the street was still dark and she could see the baker through the window shaping loaves in the same rhythm he had used for thirty years
The canoe drifted without paddling, carried by a current so gentle it barely wrinkled the surface, past banks of tall grass where red-winged blackbirds perched and called
He sanded the piece of wood along the grain, back and forth, the sawdust collecting in a fine pile on the workbench, each pass revealing a slightly smoother surface
The hospital room was quiet for the first time all day and she adjusted the blanket around her sleeping daughter and sat in the recliner and just breathed
Rain on the metal roof of the cabin made a sound like thousands of tiny fingers drumming and he lay in bed listening to it with no intention of getting up
The puzzle was nearly finished, only the sky pieces left, all similar shades of blue, and she tried each one without frustration, turning it, setting it aside, reaching for the next
The morning ferry crossed the bay without hurrying and he stood on the deck with his hands on the railing watching the wake spread out behind them in two long white lines
She watered each houseplant with a small copper can, tipping it carefully, the water darkening the soil, moving from the fern to the pothos to the spider plant by the window
The campfire had burned down to coals and nobody was talking anymore and the only light was the orange glow and the stars above the treeline
He sat on the porch steps shelling peas into a bowl, the pods splitting open with a faint pop, the peas rolling to the bottom in small green clusters
The yoga instructor said nothing for a full minute and the room held the pose together, twenty people breathing in unison, the wooden floor warm under their bare feet
She drove through the tunnel and on the other side the valley opened up green and wide and the road curved gently downhill and there were no other cars for miles
The old man swept the sidewalk in front of his shop every morning at the same time and the bristles made a soft sound on the concrete that the passing joggers never noticed
Sunday afternoon and the house was clean and the groceries were put away and there was nothing on the calendar until Monday morning and he sat down with no plan at all
The librarian stamped the books and stacked them on the cart and the only sound in the room was the turning of pages and the occasional shift of a chair
He stirred the soup slowly, the wooden spoon making a gentle circle, steam rising and curling, the kitchen windows fogged at the edges
The tide was coming in so slowly it was impossible to see it move but every few minutes the waterline was a little higher on the sand and the shells were a little more submerged
She opened the window and the night air came in cool and smelled like jasmine from the trellis below and the curtain moved once and went still
The barber clipped his hair in small even strokes, not talking, and the scissors made a rhythmic sound and the hair fell onto the black cape in tiny crescents
After the storm the air was clear and washed and the puddles reflected the sky and the birds came back to the feeder one by one
He watched the potter's wheel turn and the clay rise between her hands, the form appearing from nothing with each slow rotation, the water glistening on its surface
The train rocked gently on the tracks and the landscape outside moved at a pace that let him see every tree and every fence post and every cow standing in every field
She lay in the grass at the edge of the orchard and watched the clouds change shape above her, the bees working the clover nearby in their steady purposeful way
